---
title: "DP4: GenAI Literacy"
author: "Dr. Nikolas Johnston"
date: "May, 2026"
format:
  html:
    from: markdown+emoji
---

```{=html}
<style>
  .rubric-table table { font-size: 0.9em; border-collapse: collapse; width: 100%; }
  .rubric-table th, .rubric-table td { border: 1px solid #ccc; padding: 8px; vertical-align: top; }
  .rubric-table th { background: #2c3e50; color: white; }
  .compare-grid { display: grid; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr; gap: 12px; margin: 12px 0; }
  .compare-box { padding: 12px; border-radius: 6px; }
  .compare-weak { background: #fdf0f0; border-left: 4px solid #e74c3c; }
  .compare-strong { background: #f0fdf4; border-left: 4px solid #27ae60; }
  .compare-ai { background: #f0f7ff; border-left: 4px solid #3498db; }
  .compare-human { background: #faf5ff; border-left: 4px solid #8e44ad; }
  .prompt-type-grid { display: grid; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr; gap: 15px; margin: 15px 0; }
  .prompt-card { border-radius: 8px; padding: 15px; }
  .prompt-context { background: #eaf2f8; border: 1px solid #2874a6; }
  .prompt-do { background: #e8f8f5; border: 1px solid #1abc9c; }
  .prompt-evaluate { background: #fef9e7; border: 1px solid #b7950b; }
  .prompt-teach { background: #f5eef8; border: 1px solid #7d3c98; }
  .scenario-box { border: 2px solid #e74c3c; padding: 15px; margin: 15px 0; border-radius: 8px; background: #fdf0f0; }
  .ethical-check { background: #f0fdf4; border: 1px solid #27ae60; padding: 15px; border-radius: 8px; margin: 10px 0; }
  .practice-box { border: 2px dashed #8e44ad; padding: 15px; margin: 15px 0; border-radius: 8px; background: #faf5ff; }
  .quiz-btn { background: #2c3e50; color: white; border: none; padding: 8px 16px; border-radius: 4px; cursor: pointer; margin: 4px 2px; }
  .quiz-btn:hover { background: #3498db; }
  select.quiz-select { padding: 6px 10px; border-radius: 4px; border: 1px solid #ccc; font-size: 1em; }
  .step-badge { display: inline-block; background: #2c3e50; color: white; border-radius: 50%; width: 28px; height: 28px; text-align: center; line-height: 28px; font-weight: bold; margin-right: 8px; }
  .timer-box { background: #ffeaa7; padding: 10px 15px; border-radius: 6px; font-weight: bold; margin: 10px 0; }
  .authorship-spectrum { display: flex; margin: 15px 0; border-radius: 6px; overflow: hidden; font-size: 0.85em; }
  .spectrum-zone { padding: 12px 8px; text-align: center; flex: 1; }
  .zone-green { background: #d5f5e3; border-right: 2px solid white; }
  .zone-amber { background: #fdebd0; border-right: 2px solid white; }
  .zone-red { background: #fadbd8; }
</style>
```

# Welcome to Dry Practical 4

::: {.callout-note title="Learning Objectives"}
By the end of this session, you will be able to:

1.  **Distinguish** between AI use that supports your learning and AI use that replaces it
2.  **Construct** effective prompts using four prompt types (Context, Do-for-me, Evaluate, Teach-me) tailored to scientific writing
3.  **Revise** your own scientific writing using AI as a tool while retaining your voice and accountability
4.  **Reflect** critically on the differences between human peer feedback and AI feedback
:::

::: {.callout-important title="Why does this matter?"}
Generative AI tools like Microsoft Copilot, ChatGPT, and Google Gemini are now part of the scientific landscape. Researchers use them to refine drafts, check logic, and brainstorm — but they also produce confident-sounding text that is sometimes factually wrong, structurally misleading, or scientifically shallow.

Meanwhile, research shows students often struggle to identify where acceptable AI use ends and misconduct begins. [Chan (2024)](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10639-024-13151-7) surveyed 393 university students and found a clear gradient: students readily identified direct AI copy-paste as misconduct, but when scenarios involved editing, verifying, and rewriting AI output, agreement dropped sharply. This practical is designed to help you navigate that uncertain middle ground — developing your understanding of the ethical use of AI so you can make those judgements with confidence.
:::

::: {.callout-note title="Before you begin"}
Have the following ready:

1.  Your draft scientific report (ideally with peer review comments from DP3)
2.  A new Microsoft Word document to record your answers
3.  Microsoft Copilot or another AI tool of your choice open in a browser tab (link below)
4.  **Tracked changes** enabled in Word or Google Docs
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Part 1: The authorship line — when does AI use become misconduct? {#part1}

Before you touch a prompt, you need a framework for thinking about *when* AI use is appropriate and when it crosses a line. This is not a simple binary — it is a spectrum, and where you fall on it depends on whether you maintained intellectual control over the work.

## The Authorship Spectrum

Chan (2024) developed a 12-point AI-giarism scale that maps AI use scenarios from fully original work down to direct copy-paste. Her data from 393 students revealed behaviours at the extremes were easy to judge, but scenarios in the middle *grey* zone were much more uncertain.

Below we have prepared a spectrum that groups these scenarios into three zones. As you work through the tasks that follow, you are practising your evaluative judgement of the type of use that is often the most difficult to classify as ethical or not.

```{=html}
<div style="margin: 20px 0;">

  <!-- GREEN ZONE -->
  <div style="background: #d5f5e3; border-radius: 8px 8px 0 0; padding: 16px; border-bottom: 3px solid #27ae60;">
    <div style="display: flex; justify-content: space-between; align-items: center; margin-bottom: 10px;">
      <strong style="font-size: 1.1em; color: #1e8449;">✓ AI supports your thinking — YOU ARE THE AUTHOR</strong>
      <span style="background: #27ae60; color: white; padding: 4px 10px; border-radius: 12px; font-size: 0.8em;">Chan's F8–F12 · Students generally agree not misconduct</span>
    </div>
    <div style="display: grid; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr; gap: 8px; font-size: 0.88em;">
      <div style="background: rgba(255,255,255,0.6); padding: 10px; border-radius: 6px;">
        <strong> F12.</strong> You completed the assessment without the use of any AI tools. 
      </div>
      <div style="background: rgba(255,255,255,0.6); padding: 10px; border-radius: 6px;">
        <strong>F10–F11.</strong> You use AI to check grammar, or as a search engine for relevant sources— no AI  generated text appears in your submission.
      </div>
      <div style="background: rgba(255,255,255,0.6); padding: 10px; border-radius: 6px;">
        <strong>F9.</strong> You write your own draft, seek feedback from AI, and make improvements based on its suggestions. You acknowledged AI use.
      </div>
      <div style="background: rgba(255,255,255,0.6); padding: 10px; border-radius: 6px;">
        <strong>F8.</strong> You use AI to rephrase some of your own written content to improve writing quality. You acknowledged AI use.
      </div>
    </div>
  </div>

  <!-- AMBER ZONE -->
  <div style="background: #fdebd0; padding: 16px; border-bottom: 3px solid #e67e22;">
    <div style="display: flex; justify-content: space-between; align-items: center; margin-bottom: 10px;">
      <strong style="font-size: 1.1em; color: #b7950b;">⚠ Grey zone — support or substitution? — CAUTION</strong>
      <span style="background: #e67e22; color: white; padding: 4px 10px; border-radius: 12px; font-size: 0.8em;">Chan's F3–F7 · Students are uncertain if misconduct</span>
    </div>
    <div style="display: grid; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr; gap: 8px; font-size: 0.88em;">
      <div style="background: rgba(255,255,255,0.6); padding: 10px; border-radius: 6px;">
        <strong>F7.</strong> AI generates initial ideas, you supplement with your own. After verifying facts, you <em>rewrote most sections</em> and submitted the work as your own. You acknowledged AI use.
      </div>
      <div style="background: rgba(255,255,255,0.6); padding: 10px; border-radius: 6px;">
        <strong>F6.</strong> AI generates initial ideas, you supplement with your own. After verifying facts, you submitted work <em>with parts generated by AI and parts written by you</em>. You acknowledged AI use.
      </div>
      <div style="background: rgba(255,255,255,0.6); padding: 10px; border-radius: 6px;">
        <strong>F4–F5.</strong> You began with your own ideas, used AI to produce multiple responses, verified facts, then <em>selected the best parts</em>, made edits, and submitted. You acknowledged AI use.
      </div>
      <div style="background: rgba(255,255,255,0.6); padding: 10px; border-radius: 6px;">
        <strong>F3.</strong> You prompted AI, verified all facts, made edits, added references, formatted the response, and submitted it as your own. You acknowledged AI use.<br><em>The question: did you do the thinking, or just the editing?</em>
      </div>
    </div>
  </div>

  <!-- RED ZONE -->
  <div style="background: #fadbd8; border-radius: 0 0 8px 8px; padding: 16px;">
    <div style="display: flex; justify-content: space-between; align-items: center; margin-bottom: 10px;">
      <strong style="font-size: 1.1em; color: #c0392b;">✗ AI substitutes for your thinking — YOU ARE NOT THE AUTHOR</strong>
      <span style="background: #e74c3c; color: white; padding: 4px 10px; border-radius: 12px; font-size: 0.8em;">Chan's F1–F2 · Students agree: this is misconduct</span>
    </div>
    <div style="display: grid; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr; gap: 8px; font-size: 0.88em;">
      <div style="background: rgba(255,255,255,0.6); padding: 10px; border-radius: 6px;">
        <strong>F1.</strong> You prompted AI, <em>copied the generated response</em>, and submitted it as your own work. You acknowledged AI use.
      </div>
      <div style="background: rgba(255,255,255,0.6); padding: 10px; border-radius: 6px;">
        <strong>F2.</strong> You used AI to <em>paraphrase texts from other sources</em> without acknowledging those sources. You acknowledged AI use.
      </div>
    </div>
    <p style="font-size: 0.88em; margin-top: 10px; margin-bottom: 0;"><strong>Note:</strong> In both cases the student acknowledged using AI — but acknowledgement alone does not make it acceptable. The intellectual work was outsourced, and the student cannot take accountability for content they did not create or verify.</p>
  </div>

</div>
```

::: {.callout-important title="The accountability test"}
You can apply a simple test to any sentence in your report:

**Could you explain and defend this sentence if asked — including why you chose these words, what evidence supports this claim, and where the data came from?**

If the answer is no — if there are sentences you do not fully understand, claims you have not verified, or references you have not read — then you have lost authorship, regardless of how much of the text you technically typed.
:::

## Task: Where Does Each Scenario Fall?

Read each scenario and decide where it falls on the authorship spectrum. Check your answer, then discuss with your neighbour.

### Scenario 1

> A student asks Copilot: "Rewrite my discussion to sound more academic." The AI produces a polished version. The student reads it, thinks "this sounds much better than mine," and pastes it into their report with no changes.

<select id="s1" class="quiz-select"> <option value="">-- Select --</option> <option value="author">You are the author</option> <option value="grey">Grey zone</option> <option value="not_author">NOT the author</option> </select> <button class="quiz-btn" onclick="checkScenario('s1', 'not_author', 'sfb1')">Check</button>

::: {#sfb1 style="margin-top: 8px;"}
:::

### Scenario 2

> A student asks Copilot: "What are the key features of a strong discussion section in a biology report?" The AI explains that discussions should link results to hypotheses, compare findings with published literature, and acknowledge limitations. The student uses these principles to restructure their own discussion, writing every sentence themselves.

<select id="s2" class="quiz-select"> <option value="">-- Select --</option> <option value="author">You are the author</option> <option value="grey">Grey zone</option> <option value="not_author">NOT the author</option> </select> <button class="quiz-btn" onclick="checkScenario('s2', 'author', 'sfb2')">Check</button>

::: {#sfb2 style="margin-top: 8px;"}
:::

### Scenario 3

> A student asks Copilot to "add relevant references" to their discussion. The AI inserts three citations, including "Zhang et al. (2019)" which claims to show mitochondrial dysfunction in a related cell line. The student includes the citation without searching for the paper. The paper does not exist.

<select id="s3" class="quiz-select"> <option value="">-- Select --</option> <option value="author">You are the author</option> <option value="grey">Grey zone</option> <option value="not_author">NOT the author</option> </select> <button class="quiz-btn" onclick="checkScenario('s3', 'not_author', 'sfb3')">Check</button>

::: {#sfb3 style="margin-top: 8px;"}
:::

### Scenario 4

> A student pastes their methods section into Copilot and asks: "Is there enough detail here for someone to replicate my experiment?" The AI responds that the centrifugation speed and duration are missing. The student adds those details in their own words.

<select id="s4" class="quiz-select"> <option value="">-- Select --</option> <option value="author">You are the author</option> <option value="grey">Grey zone</option> <option value="not_author">NOT the author</option> </select> <button class="quiz-btn" onclick="checkScenario('s4', 'author', 'sfb4')">Check</button>

::: {#sfb4 style="margin-top: 8px;"}
:::

### Scenario 5

> A student asks Copilot to rewrite their results paragraph. The AI version is more concise and better structured. The student compares both versions carefully, takes two phrases from the AI version, rewrites the rest themselves, and checks that all data values match their original spreadsheet.

<select id="s5" class="quiz-select"> <option value="">-- Select --</option> <option value="author">You are the author</option> <option value="grey">Grey zone</option> <option value="not_author">NOT the author</option> </select> <button class="quiz-btn" onclick="checkScenario('s5', 'grey', 'sfb5')">Check</button>

::: {#sfb5 style="margin-top: 8px;"}
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="Discuss with your neighbour"}
Were there any scenarios where you disagreed? What made the difference between "author" and "grey zone"? What is the minimum amount of critical evaluation needed before you can claim authorship of a revised sentence?
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Part 2: Catching AI errors {#part5}

AI tools are fluent but not reliable. They produce text that *sounds* correct even when it is wrong. This is not a theoretical concern — it is empirically documented at alarming rates.Even elite peer review fails to catch these errors. If expert scientists miss these errors, you should assume you will too — unless you **verify** every AI-generated claim.

## Task: Spot the Problem

Each example below shows AI-generated text produced in response to a student's prompt. Identify what went wrong.

### Example 1:

> **Student prompt:** "Add a reference to support my claim that citrate synthase is a mitochondrial matrix enzyme."
>
> **AI output:** "Citrate synthase is a well-established marker of the mitochondrial matrix (Hargreaves & Bhatt, 2020). Its activity is commonly used to assess mitochondrial content and integrity in both clinical and research settings."

<select id="e1" class="quiz-select"> <option value="">-- What is the problem? --</option> <option value="grammar">Grammar error</option> <option value="hallucination">Hallucinated reference</option> <option value="interpretation">Over-interpretation</option> <option value="none">No problem</option> </select> <button class="quiz-btn" onclick="checkError('e1', 'hallucination', 'efb1')">Check</button>

::: {#efb1 style="margin-top: 8px;"}
:::

### Example 2:

> **Student's original:** "Citrate synthase activity was lower in the diseased cell line compared to the healthy control, which may indicate impaired mitochondrial function in this model."
>
> **AI rewrite:** "Citrate synthase activity was significantly reduced in diseased cells, confirming mitochondrial dysfunction as a hallmark of this disease."

<select id="e2" class="quiz-select"> <option value="">-- What is the problem? --</option> <option value="grammar">Grammar error</option> <option value="hallucination">Hallucinated reference</option> <option value="interpretation">Over-interpretation — cautious language replaced with definitive claims</option> <option value="none">No problem</option> </select> <button class="quiz-btn" onclick="checkError('e2', 'interpretation', 'efb2')">Check</button>

::: {#efb2 style="margin-top: 8px;"}
:::

### Example 3:

> **Student prompt:** "Make my results section more detailed. Citrate synthase specific activity in the mitochondrial fraction was increased in healthy cells and decreased in diseased cells (p = 0.003, Student's t-test)."
>
> **AI output:** "Citrate synthase specific activity in the mitochondrial fraction was 45.2 ± 2.8 nmol/min/mg in healthy cells and 31.6 ± 3.4 nmol/min/mg in diseased cells (p = 0.003, Student's t-test)."

<select id="e3" class="quiz-select"> <option value="">-- What is the problem? --</option> <option value="fabrication">AI may have fabricated specific numerical values</option> <option value="structure">Wrong section structure</option> <option value="tone">Inappropriate tone</option> <option value="none">No problem</option> </select> <button class="quiz-btn" onclick="checkError('e3', 'fabrication', 'efb3')">Check</button>

::: {#efb3 style="margin-top: 8px;"}
:::

::: {.callout-warning title="The verification checklist — accountability in practice"}
Every time you use AI-generated text in your report, apply the accountability test. Can you defend each of these?

1.  **References** — Search for every citation the AI provides in PubMed or Google Scholar. Does the paper exist? Does it say what the AI claims?
2.  **Data values** — Cross-check every number against your original data.
3.  **Strength of claims** — Has the AI upgraded "may suggest" to "confirms" or "demonstrates"? Your undergraduate experiment with limited replication almost certainly does not support definitive claims.
4.  **Added content** — Has the AI introduced information, mechanisms, or comparisons that were not in your original text? If you cannot verify them against a source you have read, remove them.
5.  **Removed qualifiers** — Has the AI stripped out important caveats like "in this experiment" or "under these conditions"?
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Part 3: Prompt engineering for scientific writing and hands on report revision {#part2}

Not all prompts are equal. The way you talk to an AI tool determines the quality of what you get back. In this section you will learn four prompt types, practise each one on your own report, and critically evaluate the results.

## The Four Prompt Types

```{=html}
<div class="prompt-type-grid">
  <div class="prompt-card prompt-context">
    <h4 style="color: #2874a6; margin-top: 0;">🧠 Context Prompt</h4>
    <p><strong>Purpose:</strong> Set the AI's role so its output matches your discipline and audience.</p>
    <p><strong>Example:</strong> <em>"You are a biology lecturer who marks third-year scientific reports on enzyme biochemistry. The reports follow a standard report structure."</em></p>
    <p style="margin-bottom:0;"><strong>Why it works:</strong> Without context, the AI defaults to generic advice. With it, feedback can be calibrated to your specific task and standard.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="prompt-card prompt-do">
    <h4 style="color: #1abc9c; margin-top: 0;">🔧 "Do for me" Prompt</h4>
    <p><strong>Purpose:</strong> Delegate a specific writing task to the AI.</p>
    <p><strong>Example:</strong> <em>"Rewrite the following results paragraph so that it reports only what the data show, without interpretation. Keep all numerical values and statistical tests intact."</em></p>
    <p style="margin-bottom:0;"><strong>Why it works:</strong> Specific constraints produce better output. <strong>Caution:</strong> You must critically evaluate the result — the AI may alter data, change meaning, or introduce errors. If you cannot articulate <em>why</em> the AI's version is better than yours, you have not learned from the process.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="prompt-card prompt-evaluate">
    <h4 style="color: #b7950b; margin-top: 0;">🔍 "Evaluate" Prompt</h4>
    <p><strong>Purpose:</strong> Ask the AI to critique your writing rather than rewrite it.</p>
    <p><strong>Example:</strong> <em>"Read my discussion section. Does my interpretation of the citrate synthase data logically follow from the results I reported? Identify any logical gaps or unsupported claims."</em></p>
    <p style="margin-bottom:0;"><strong>Why it works:</strong> This supports metacognition — you remain in control of the writing, but gain an external perspective on its logic and structure.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="prompt-card prompt-teach">
    <h4 style="color: #7d3c98; margin-top: 0;">🎓 "Teach me" Prompt</h4>
    <p><strong>Purpose:</strong> Deepen your understanding of a writing principle or scientific concept.</p>
    <p><strong>Example:</strong> <em>"Explain the difference between reporting results and interpreting results in a scientific report. Give me an example using citrate synthase activity data."</em></p>
    <p style="margin-bottom:0;"><strong>Why it works:</strong> This builds your skill rather than outsourcing it. The AI teaches you a principle you then apply yourself.</p>
  </div>
</div>
```

You will now apply each prompt type to your own scientific report. For each step, record your prompt, the AI output, your evaluation, and what you changed. This forms the basis of your Assessment 5 Pro Forma.

::: {.callout-important title="Critical rule"}
**Do not paste your entire report into the AI.** Work with one paragraph or section at a time. This gives you more control, makes evaluation easier, and reduces the risk of the AI fabricating connections between sections.
:::

## Step 1: Set the Context

Write a context prompt that tells the AI who it is and what it is reviewing. Use this as your opening message before any other prompts.

::: practice-box
**Your context prompt:**

Write a prompt that specifies:

-   The AI's role (e.g., biology lecturer, journal reviewer, scientific editor)
-   The type of document (third-year scientific report)
-   The subject matter (citrate synthase activity in subcellular fractions)
-   The assessment criteria (clarity, accuracy, evidence-based reasoning, IMRaD structure)

Record your prompt in your Word document.
:::

## Step 2: "Do for me" — Targeted Revision

Choose a paragraph from your report that you know needs improvement. Paste it into Copilot with a specific "do for me" prompt.

::: practice-box
**Before you accept anything the AI returns, check:**

-   [ ] Are all numerical values (means, SDs, p-values) unchanged?
-   [ ] Has the AI added any claims or interpretations not in your original?
-   [ ] Has the AI removed any important qualifying language (e.g., "in this experiment" → "universally")?
-   [ ] Does the revised text still mean what *you* intended it to mean?
-   [ ] If the AI suggested references, have you verified each one exists?

Record: your prompt, the AI output, what you changed, and what you rejected.
:::

## Step 3: "Evaluate" — Getting a Critique

Choose a different section (ideally your discussion) and ask the AI to evaluate it rather than rewrite it.

::: practice-box
**Good evaluate prompts for biology reports:**

-   "Does my interpretation of \[specific result\] logically follow from the data I presented in my results section?"
-   "Are there any claims in this paragraph that are not supported by the data or references I cite?"
-   "Does this discussion section address the original hypothesis stated in my introduction?"

Record: your prompt, the AI's critique, and what action you took in response.
:::

## Step 4: "Teach me" — Building Your Understanding

Ask the AI to explain a writing or science concept you found difficult during this report.

::: practice-box
**Example teach-me prompts:**

-   "What is the difference between a result and an interpretation in a biology report? Give me an example using enzyme activity data."
-   "How should I structure a discussion paragraph that compares my findings to published literature?"
-   "Why is it important to normalise enzyme activity to protein content, and how should I explain this in my methods?"

Record: your prompt, the key insight you gained, and how it changed your approach.
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Part 4: Assessment 5 Walkthrough {#part6}

Everything you have practised today feeds directly into Assessment 5. This section steps you through the Pro Forma requirements.

::: {.callout-important title="Download required"}
Download the Assessment 5 Pro Forma from the BIOL340 Moodle page. The sections below map directly onto the Pro Forma structure.
:::

## [1]{.step-badge} Human Feedback Summary

Using the peer review feedback you received in DP3/FeedbackFruits:

::: practice-box
**List 1–3 key points of feedback from your peer reviewer.** Be specific — quote or paraphrase the feedback and note which section of your report it applies to.

1.  ...
2.  ...
3.  ...

**For each point, describe how you will address it in your revision.**
:::

## [2]{.step-badge} AI-Assisted Revision

Using the four prompt types, revise your report. For each prompt type, record the following in your Pro Forma:

### 2.1 Context Prompt

-   The prompt you used to set the AI's role
-   Why you chose that framing

### 2.2 "Do for me" Prompt

-   The prompt you used
-   The AI's output (paste it in)
-   What you changed in your report as a result — and what you rejected

### 2.3 "Evaluate" Prompt

-   The prompt you used
-   The AI's critique
-   What action you took in response

### 2.4 "Teach me" Prompt

-   The prompt you used
-   The key insight you gained
-   How it changed your approach to writing

## [3]{.step-badge} Reflection (100–150 words each, 600 words max)

Answer each question in the Pro Forma:

### 3.1 Compare AI & Human Feedback

> How did AI feedback compare with your peer's feedback? Which was more useful for which aspects of your writing?

### 3.2 Most Effective Prompt Type

> Which of the four prompt types helped you most? Why?

### 3.3 Impact on Your Writing Process

> Did using AI change how you approach writing or editing? What will you do differently next time?

### 3.4 Benefits and Risks

> What are the benefits and risks of using GenAI in scientific writing? What safeguards should a scientist apply?

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Final Checklist

::: {.callout-important title="Before submitting — the accountability check"}
Confirm each item:

-   [ ] Tracked changes enabled in your revised report showing all modifications
-   [ ] Completed Pro Forma with feedback summary, all four prompt types, and full reflection
-   [ ] Screenshots or transcripts of your AI interactions included in the Appendix
-   [ ] All AI-suggested references independently verified in PubMed or Google Scholar (or removed if unverifiable)
-   [ ] All numerical values cross-checked against your original data
-   [ ] Accountability test passed: Every sentence in your final report is one you can explain, defend, and take responsibility for

:outbox_tray: **Upload to Moodle using the feedback fruits link for Assessment 5**
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Prompt Vault

Record your most effective prompt from today's session. These will be shared across the class as a collective resource.

::::: {.padlet-embed style="border:1px solid rgba(0,0,0,0.1);border-radius:2px;box-sizing:border-box;overflow:hidden;position:relative;width:100%;background:#F4F4F4"}
<p style="padding:0;margin:0">

<iframe src="https://padlet.com/embed/91492p0ab0lx1fyl" frameborder="0" allow="camera;microphone;geolocation;display-capture;clipboard-write" style="width:100%;height:608px;display:block;padding:0;margin:0">

</iframe>

</p>

:::: {style="display:flex;align-items:center;justify-content:end;margin:0;height:28px"}
<a href="https://padlet.com?ref=embed" style="display:block;flex-grow:0;margin:0;border:none;padding:0;text-decoration:none" target="_blank">

::: {style="display:flex;align-items:center;"}
<img src="https://padlet.net/embeds/made_with_padlet_2022.png" alt="Made with Padlet" width="114" height="28" style="padding:0;margin:0;background:0 0;border:none;box-shadow:none"/>
:::

</a>
::::
:::::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

> **Final thought:** AI is a tool — you are the scientist.The value of your degree is not that you can produce polished text. It is that you can *think critically, evaluate evidence, and communicate your reasoning*. No AI can do that for you. Own your writing, verify your claims, and never submit anything you cannot defend.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Useful References

Bozkurt, A. (2024). GenAI et al.: Cocreation, authorship, ownership, academic ethics and integrity in a time of generative AI. *Open Praxis*, 16(1), 1–10. https://doi.org/10.55982/openpraxis.16.1.654

Chan, C.K.Y. (2024). Students' perceptions of 'AI-giarism': Investigating changes in understandings of academic misconduct. *Education and Information Technologies*, 30(6), 8087–8108. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10639-024-13151-7

Jackson, J. (2025). Higher order prompting: Applying Bloom's revised taxonomy to the use of large language models in higher education. *Studies in Technology Enhanced Learning*, 4(1), 1–17. https://doi.org/10.21428/8c225f6e.0915c17e

```{=html}
<script>
  const scenarioFeedback = {
    'author': "<strong style='color:#27ae60;'>You are the author — AI supported your thinking.</strong> The student maintained human agency throughout: they did the intellectual work (reasoning, writing, decision-making) themselves. The AI functioned as a support tool — like asking a tutor for advice — not as a substitute for thinking.,
    'grey': "<strong style='color:#e67e22;'>Grey zone — support or substitution?</strong> The student engaged critically with the AI output — comparing, selecting, editing, and verifying — but some of the final text originated with the AI. It can be acceptable if the student can genuinely defend every sentence, but requires honest self-assessment: did you do the thinking, or did the AI?",
    'not_author': "<strong style='color:#e74c3c;'>You are NOT the author — AI substituted for your thinking.</strong> The student outsourced the intellectual work to the AI. Whether it was accepting unverified text, including fabricated citations, or submitting AI-written text without critical evaluation.
  };

  const promptFeedback = {
    'correct': "<strong style='color:#27ae60;'>✓ Correct!</strong> The stronger prompt provides context, specifies the task precisely, and constrains the AI's output. This reduces hallucination and gives you a result you can actually evaluate critically.",
    'incorrect': "<strong style='color:#e74c3c;'>✗ Not quite.</strong> Vague prompts give the AI too much freedom, leading to generic output, hallucinated content, and meaning drift. The more specific and constrained your prompt, the more useful and verifiable the output."
  };

  const errorFeedback = {
    'hallucination': "<div style='padding:10px; background:#fadbd8; border-radius:6px;'><strong style='color:#e74c3c;'>Hallucinated reference.</strong> AI tools frequently generate citations that look real but do not exist. Naser, 2026 found fabrication rates of 11–57% across models, with even higher rates for specialised topics. \"Hargreaves & Bhatt (2020)\" may sound plausible, but you must search for every reference the AI suggests in PubMed or Google Scholar. If you cannot find it, it was fabricated. <strong>Never include a reference you have not personally located and read.</strong></div>",
    'interpretation': "<div style='padding:10px; background:#fadbd8; border-radius:6px;'><strong style='color:#e74c3c;'>Over-interpretation.</strong> The student's original used careful language: \"may indicate\" and \"in this model.\" The AI replaced this with \"confirming\" and \"hallmark\" — definitive claims the data do not support. A single experiment showing reduced activity does not <em>confirm</em> dysfunction or establish a <em>hallmark</em>. This kind of subtle meaning shift is one of the most dangerous AI errors because it sounds more confident and therefore \"better.\" If you cannot defend the strength of a claim from your own data, the AI has substituted its confident-sounding language for your scientific judgement.</div>",
    'fabrication': "<div style='padding:10px; background:#fadbd8; border-radius:6px;'><strong style='color:#e74c3c;'>Likely fabricated data.</strong> The AI cannot access your spreadsheet, your lab notebook, or your statistical output. When asked to \"make results more detailed,\" it may invent plausible-looking numbers, p-values, and statistical tests. This is the AI equivalent of data fabrication — a serious form of research misconduct. <strong>Every number in your results section must come from your actual data.</strong> If you did not calculate it, it does not belong in your report.</div>",
    'grammar': "<div style='padding:10px; background:#fdebd0; border-radius:6px;'><strong style='color:#e67e22;'>Not the main issue here.</strong> Look again — the problem is more fundamental than grammar.</div>",
    'structure': "<div style='padding:10px; background:#fdebd0; border-radius:6px;'><strong style='color:#e67e22;'>Not quite.</strong> The structure is fine — the problem is with the content itself.</div>",
    'tone': "<div style='padding:10px; background:#fdebd0; border-radius:6px;'><strong style='color:#e67e22;'>The tone is appropriate for scientific writing.</strong> Look more carefully at what the AI actually produced.</div>",
    'none': "<div style='padding:10px; background:#fadbd8; border-radius:6px;'><strong style='color:#e74c3c;'>There is a problem here.</strong> Look again at what the AI produced and ask: can you verify this content against your own data and the published literature?</div>"
  };

  function checkScenario(selectId, correctAnswer, feedbackId) {
    const selected = document.getElementById(selectId).value;
    const box = document.getElementById(feedbackId);
    if (selected === "") {
      box.innerHTML = "<em>Please select an answer.</em>";
    } else if (selected === correctAnswer) {
      box.innerHTML = "<div style='padding:10px; background:#d5f5e3; border-radius:6px; margin-top:6px;'><strong style='color:#27ae60;'>✓ Correct!</strong> " + scenarioFeedback[selected] + "</div>";
    } else {
      box.innerHTML = "<div style='padding:10px; background:#fadbd8; border-radius:6px; margin-top:6px;'><strong style='color:#e74c3c;'>✗ Not quite.</strong> This scenario is best classified as: " + scenarioFeedback[correctAnswer] + "</div>";
    }
  }

  function checkPrompt(selectId, correctAnswer, feedbackId) {
    const selected = document.getElementById(selectId).value;
    const box = document.getElementById(feedbackId);
    if (selected === "") {
      box.innerHTML = "<em>Please select an answer.</em>";
    } else if (selected === correctAnswer) {
      box.innerHTML = "<div style='padding:10px; background:#d5f5e3; border-radius:6px;'>" + promptFeedback['correct'] + "</div>";
    } else {
      box.innerHTML = "<div style='padding:10px; background:#fadbd8; border-radius:6px;'>" + promptFeedback['incorrect'] + "</div>";
    }
  }

  function checkError(selectId, correctAnswer, feedbackId) {
    const selected = document.getElementById(selectId).value;
    const box = document.getElementById(feedbackId);
    if (selected === "") {
      box.innerHTML = "<em>Please select an answer.</em>";
    } else if (selected === correctAnswer) {
      box.innerHTML = "<div style='padding:10px; background:#d5f5e3; border-radius:6px;'><strong style='color:#27ae60;'>✓ Correct!</strong><br>" + errorFeedback[selected] + "</div>";
    } else {
      box.innerHTML = errorFeedback[selected] || "<div style='padding:10px; background:#fadbd8; border-radius:6px;'><strong style='color:#e74c3c;'>✗ Not quite.</strong><br>" + errorFeedback[correctAnswer] + "</div>";
    }
  }
</script>
```
